coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposite for electrocatalytic application of L-cysteine in whole blood and live cells
coredata.dc:creator: Arul, P.
Huang, Sheng-Tung
Mani, Veerappan
Huang, Chih-Hung
coredata.dc:description: Driven by the urgent need to detect amino acids in live cells at trace levels, as they are an important biomarker for various diseases. For this purpose, we have developed a nanomolar level detection of l-cysteine (L-Cys) and real-time cellular quantification from live cells based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-AuNPs-metal-free organic framework (MFOF). Different analytical and microscopic techniques were used to confirm the synthesized nanocomposite. The sensitive electrochemical sensing system of l-Cys-was detected synergistically with GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE by voltammetry and amperometric i-t curve. The electrocatalytic oxidation signal of l-Cys-at E pa = none, +0.45, +0.36 and +0.21 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with enhanced current responses were observed at control GCE, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF, and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs by DPV. The nanocomposite modified sensor probe showed a wide linear range concentration from 1 nM to 0.5 mM with LOD of 0.124 nM (S/N = 3), LOQ of 0.863 nM (S/N = 10) and sensitivity of 2.364 μA μM−1 cm−2. Subsequently, the selective detection of l-Cys-in the presence of possible other interfering biothiols. amino acids, and metal ions were achieved. The practical application was demonstrated in human whole blood and bio-fluid samples. Finally, the endogenous real-time detection of cellular cysteine in live cells was demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: L-cysteine
Biosensor
Electrocatalytic
Human whole blood
Cellular cysteine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay method for the growth of live cells
Assay method for the endogenous stimulation of cellular cysteine
Effect of selectivity, stability, and reproducibility
Practical application in whole blood samples
Real-time practical application in live cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan ( MOST-107–2113-M-027–006 and MOST-108–2113-M-027–001 ). P. Arul would like to gratitude National Taipei University of Technology for the position of Research Assistant Professor.

The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST-107–2113-M-027–006 and MOST-108–2113-M-027–001). P. Arul would like to gratitude National Taipei University of Technology for the position of Research Assistant Professor.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of GQDs (a), GQDs-AuNPs (b), MFOF (c), GQDs-MFOF (d), and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF (e and f).
XPS spectrum of GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF (a), Deconvoluted spectra of Cls (b), Nls (c), O1s (d), and Au4f (e).

(A) CVs for 200 µM of L-Cys-at GCE (a), MFOF (b), GQDs-MFOF (c), GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs (d) (1st cycle: solid line : 10th cycle: dotted line ) in PBS (pH 7.0) at 50 mV/s and absence of l-Cys (e). (B) Bar graph picture for modified electrodes vs. current of l-Cys-with error bar (n = 5).
(A) DPVs for each 2 µM of l-Cys-at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE (curves a-n). (B) Calibration plot for current vs. [L-Cys] with error bar (n = 5).

(A) Amperometric i-t curves for l-Cys-at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE. Each addition increases the concentration of 3 nM of l-Cys-at a regular sample interval of 50 s. (B) Plot of current vs. [L-Cys]. (C and D) Wide range concentration of l-Cys (a) 1 (b) 3 (c) 5 (d) 10 (e) 50 (f) 100 (g) 250 (h) 500 nM (i) 1 (j) 50, (k) 100, (l) 250, (m) 500, (n) 750, (o) 1000 µM (p) 0.2, (q) 0.3, and (r) 0.5 mM (Plot of current vs. [L-Cys] (inset)). (E) Amperometric i-t curve for 5 nM of l-Cys (a), 50 nM of homocysteine (b), glutathione (c), tyrosine (d), glucose (e), urea (f), thiourea (g), tryptophan (h) and 50-fold higher concentrations of Na+ (i), K + (j), Cd2+(k), Zn2+(l), NO3 − (m) and SO4 2−(n).

(A-B) DPVs for absence (a), presence of human whole blood samples (b) and 2 µM of l-Cys (c) at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE. (C) DPVs for E. coli W3110 (a), serine (b), NaHS (c), E. coli W3110 (serine + NaHS) (d) + E. coli W3110 (serine + NaHS + NEM) (e) in LB medium.
Schematic representation of the synthesis of GQDs, and GQDs-AuNPs.
Scheme represents the real-time production of cellular Cys-in E. coli W3110.
Comparison of the present GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE with the reported sensors against electrochemical sensing of l-Cys.

row.entry: Human whole blood and E. coli W3110

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots-based nanocomposite for electrocatalytic application of L-cysteine in whole blood and live cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Institute of Biochemical and Biomedical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan, Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology
Institute of Biochemical and Biomedical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Institute of Biochemical and Biomedical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan, Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sensors Lab, Advanced Membranes and Porous Materials Center (AMPMC), Computer Electrical Mathematical Science and Engineering (CEMSE) Division, King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Thuwal 23955-6900, Saudi Arabia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sensors Lab, Advanced Membranes and Porous Materials Center (AMPMC), Computer Electrical Mathematical Science and Engineering (CEMSE) Division,
King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSensors Lab, Advanced Membranes and Porous Materials Center (AMPMC), Computer Electrical Mathematical Science and Engineering (CEMSE) Division, King Abdullah University of Science and Technology (KAUST), Thuwal 23955-6900, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Driven by the urgent need to detect amino acids in live cells at trace levels, as they are an important biomarker for various diseases. For this purpose, we have developed a nanomolar level detection of l-cysteine (L-Cys) and real-time cellular quantification from live cells based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-AuNPs-metal-free organic framework (MFOF). Different analytical and microscopic techniques were used to confirm the synthesized nanocomposite. The sensitive electrochemical sensing system of l-Cys-was detected synergistically with GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE by voltammetry and amperometric i-t curve. The electrocatalytic oxidation signal of l-Cys-at E pa = none, +0.45, +0.36 and +0.21 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with enhanced current responses were observed at control GCE, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF, and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs by DPV. The nanocomposite modified sensor probe showed a wide linear range concentration from 1 nM to 0.5 mM with LOD of 0.124 nM (S/N = 3), LOQ of 0.863 nM (S/N = 10) and sensitivity of 2.364 μA μM−1 cm−2. Subsequently, the selective detection of l-Cys-in the presence of possible other interfering biothiols. amino acids, and metal ions were achieved. The practical application was demonstrated in human whole blood and bio-fluid samples. Finally, the endogenous real-time detection of cellular cysteine in live cells was demonstrated.

Ultrasensitive electrochemical determination of L-Cysteine in biological media using GQDs-AuNPs embedded in a metal-free organic scaffold
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomolecules are global significance in the natural system and play a prominent role in biological, clinical, biochemical, and biotechnological fields [1–3]. Intracellular biothiol-molecules including cysteine (Cys), glutathione (Gsh), and homocysteine (Hcy) are an important key factor in live cells and their responsible for controlling various biofunctions and redox reactions [4]. Among the three, Cys-is an important biothiol molecule and are essential role in several metabolizing functions [5]. It is known that neurotoxin biomarker in biological cell systems by various enzymes as cysteine proteases [6,7]. The chemical binding interactions between the protein and Cys-via disulfide regulate the secondary metabolic structures [8,9]. Cys-used as a radioprotective drug marker, radical scavenger and cancer indicator [6,7]. Based on the universal agencies, the recommended level of Cys ∼30–200 µM and it has been associated with various diseases including rheumatoid arthritis, Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases, lysosomal defects and cystinuria [10]. On the other hand, deficiency of Cys-was closely connected with metabolic derailments such as slowed growth, depigmentation of hair, liver damage, skin lesions, and fat loss. Therefore, simple detection method for l-Cys-is a crucial for the clinical and biological fields.

Various analytical methods have been developed for the detection of Cys, including colorimetric [11], Raman spectroscopy [12], HPLC [13] and so on. The most existing techniques have notable shortfalls including complex sample preparation, long detection time, high cost, need for a trained person for instrument handling, and poor of sensitivity and selectivity. To overcome the above requirements, simple and continuous detection of Cys-is more important now a days. The electrochemical sensing platform is a simple and easily distinguishable for selective target, but it still remains great challenge for the stable and ultra-sensitive determination [3,7,9]. Thus, no current signal was observed for Cys-using unmodified electrode surface (control electrode). To solve this problem, electrode surface was fabricated with an electroactive functional material and then potentially utilized for Cys-detection. So far, very few electrochemical sensors excited for l-Cys-at different hybrid nanoelectrocatalyst modified surfaces [7,14–22]. Including Fe3O4@PDA/CuxO [7], SiO2/AuNPs/SiO2 [14], rGO/b-CD [15], Cu@CuxO nanowires [16], PPy/GQDs@PB/GF [17], Au nanorods [18], NH2-functionalized BaTiO3 /NPs [19], gallium nitride nanowires [20], MnO2 nanowires [21], and GNSs/MnOx [22]. Despite this, continuous, selective, and live cell determination of l-Cys-is a still problematic, due to poor selectivity and electrode fouling. Therefore, the selection of electrocatalyst is crucial to obtain a stable sensor with higher catalytic performance.

Metal-free organic frameworks (MFOFs) or covalent organic frameworks (COFs) are highly crystalline network structure and was constructed from two lighter functional groups ligands via a strong covalent bond [23]. MFOF exhibits excellent physical and chemical properties and has been used for fuel cells, sensors, drug carriers, and gas adsorption/separation applications [23–25]. Moreover, the electrical conductivity of MFOF was still limited due to its extended chemical structures [25]. To enhance the electrical conductivity, MFOF has been potentially combined with other electroactive functional materials and to fulfill the above shortfalls [26]. In recent years, GQDs are an emerging class of new nanocarbon material and showed excellent properties due to their quantum confinement effect, biocompatibility, low toxicity, highly conductive and it was potentially used for various type of applications [17,27]. GQDs play a strong electron donors, acceptors and easy functionalization with other materials. Moreover, metal nanoparticles (MNPs) play a prominent role as an electrocatalyst due to their large surface-to-volume ratio, high electroactive surface area, and good electrical conductivity, but they have failed to provide stability in the absence of stabilizing agents [28]. There are many commercial reducing as well as stabilizing agents were used to synthesize variety of MNPs, but it is very difficult to prepare uniform diameter of particles and long-term stability. Therefore, alternative materials are now needed for the synthesis of stable and uniform MNPs without an external reducing reagent. A well-known that, GQDs have acted as both stabilizing and reducing agent for the synthesis of stable MNPs [27]. The intrinsic and combination effect of GQDs with MNPs shows excellent catalytic performance for many potential applications due to their extended functional switching properties [27].

In this work, GQDs capped AuNPs-MFOF was designed and then utilized for sensitive detection of l-Cys-and real-time cellular Cys-in live cells. The synthesized nanocarbon composite material was characterized and confirmed by FT-IR, XRD, XPS, and HR-TEM, respectively. The electrocatalytic performance of l-Cys-was monitored by cyclic and differential pulse voltammetry techniques and then sensitive determination was developed by amperometric i-t curve (amp i-t curve). The amp i-t curve result of l-Cys-showed an extended linear wide range concentration, lower LOD and highly selective. Finally, the practical application was demonstrated in whole blood and live cell samples.

A brief description of chemicals, reagents, and analytical instrumentations were included in the electronic supplementry information (ESI) (SI.1 and SI.2). The MFOF was synthesized as follows (Scheme S1) [29]. About, melamine (50 mg) (99.0%) and terephthalaldehyde (80 mg) (99%) was completely liquefied in 20 mL anhydrous dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (≥99.9%) under N2 environment. Then, the reaction mixture was transferred into a 50 mL of round bottom flask and heated at 180°C (heating rate 5ᵒC/min) for 3 days. At the end of reaction time, as resultant mixture was cooled to ideal temperature and then filtered followed by washed repeatedly with EtOH and DMSO. Finally, as synthesized MFOF material was dried in a vacuum oven at 80°C for 5 h. The GQDs was prepared as follows (Scheme 1 ) [17]. About, 1 g of l-Aspartic acid (L-Asp) was added to the silicate crucible and heated around at 210°C using a conventional heating mantle. Then sample was kept in the heating jacket until the liquid sample turned a brown color for 50 s. The resultant mixture was cooled at room temperature and 10 mL of DI-water was added, followed by sonication for 30 min to obtain a transparent solution. The obtained GQDs were further purified by ultracentrifugation at 8000 RPM for 30 min. Finally, the prepared GQDs was stored at room temperature. A detailed synthetic procedure of GQDs-AuNPs and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF are given in ESI (Scheme 1) (ESI. SI.3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay method for the growth of live cells
ce:section.ce:para: The growth of live cells (E. coil W3110) was developed based on the reported work [9]. Initially, live E. coli W3110 cells were cultured on an agar plate containing Luria broth (LB). The cells of E. coli W3110 was co-incubated overnight at 37°C. Then a single colony of cells were introduced into LB medium and the co-incubation period was further extended for another night. Then, LB medium was enriched with 1 mM, 0.5 mM and 0.25% of tryptophan, pyridoxal phosphate and butyl acetate followed by co-incubated at 37°C for 1 h. The complete growth of live E. coli W3110 cells was measured by spectrophotometer. Then, live cells were diluted with freshly prepared LB medium and cells of E. coli W3110 was calculated to be 2.5 × 106 cells/cm3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay method for the endogenous stimulation of cellular cysteine
ce:section.ce:para: The developed E. coli W3110 (2.5 × 106 cells/cm3) was completely transferred into an electrochemical working vessel containing LB medium. Then, known amounts of serine and sodium hydrogen sulfide (NaHS) was spiked into the live cells containing working medium. The E. coli W3110 cells were effectively stimulated and then endogenously released the cellular cysteine. The endogenous production of cellular cysteine was monitored by amp i-t curve with help of nanoelectrocatalyst (GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF) modified electrode surface.

Fig. S1 shows the UV–Vis spectra of l-Asp, GQDs, HAuCl4·3H2O and GQDs-AuNPs (inset). For l-Asp, no absorption peak was observed in the active wavelength window (curve a) and GQDs at 291 nm due to n-π* of the surface-functional groups of active sites (curve b) [28]. In addition, the sharp peak at 289 nm confirms the absorption behavior of HAuCl4·3H2O (curve c) [30]. Interestingly, a new peak at 541 nm, indicating the successful formation of AuNPs (inset). In absence of shoulder band at lower wavelength region reveals that GQDs are completely participated for the formation of gold nanoparticles (inset) [30,31]. Fig. 1 shows the HR-TEM images of GQDs and GQDs-AuNPs. The morphological images show small dotted particles with an average particle diameter ∼ 2.6 ± 0.05 nm (Fig. 1a). Interestingly, diameter of the particles was increased from 2.6 to 12.45 ± 0.09 nm due to their successful formation of AuNPs capped GQDs (Fig. 1b).

Fig. S2 shows the FT-IR spectra of MA, TPA, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF. A detailed description of stretching, bending vibrations and their corresponding peak assignments were given in the ESI (Table S1). The FT-IR spectrum of MA peaks at 1025, 1658 and 3468 cm−1 (Fig. S2a). The peak at 1025 cm−1 due to C-N stretching vibration and 1658 cm−1 confirms the bending of NH2 [32]. The peak at 3468 cm−1 due to strong stretching vibration of NH2 [32]. The FT-IR of TPA shows at 783 and 818 cm−1 confirm the stretching vibrations of -C=C and -C-H, respectively (Fig. S2b). The peaks at 1089 and 2870 cm−1 correspond to -C-C-O and C-H [32]. Fig. S2c shows the FT-IR of MFOF. A new band at 768 cm−1 assigned to C-H and 1268 cm−1 for the confirmation of -C=N. The peaks at 1483 and 1586 cm−1 are successful formation of the triazine ring of MFOF [33]. Curves S2d and e show the FT-IR spectra of GQDs-MFOF and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF. The GQDs-MFOF and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF showed significant peak shifts and acquired new stretching/bending vibrations are listed in Table S1. In conclusion, the FT-IR result of AuNPs capped GQDs-MFOF shows similar peaks as for GQDs-MFOF and a significant masking of -OH functional site is responsible for participation of synthesized nanoparticles (Fig. S2e). Fig. S3 shows the XRD patterns of MFOF, GQDs-MFOF and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF. The MFOF and GQDs-MFOF show broad peak at 20.75ᵒ (0 0 2), which is consistent with the crystalline nature of organic framework (curves a and b) [17,25]. In addition, the sharp and intense 2θ-peaks at 38.02°, 44.52°, 64.53°, and 77.67° are assigned to the Miller index (hkl) planes of (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), and (3 1 1) for AuNPs (JCPDS No. 41–1402) (curve c) [30,31].

XPS is an important analytical tool for the detailed investigation of electronic state and chemical bonding environment of as synthesized material. Fig. 2 a shows the XPS of GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF and the peaks show C, N, O, and Au respectively. Fig. 2b shows the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C1s, six different binding energies observed at 284.9, 285.5, 286.1, 286.8, 287.3 and 288.4 eV corresponding to –C=C, –C-C, –C-N, –C=N, –C=O and –O=C-OH, respectively [26,30,31]. On the other hand, N1s shows two different peaks at 398.4 and 399.8 eV assigned to –C=N and –NH (Fig. 2c) [33,34]. The O1s peaks at 531.3 and 533.1 eV are chemical environments probably –C=O, and –C-O (Fig. 2d) [25]. Finally, Au4f shows two different binding energies at 86.1 and 89.6 eV are closely assigned to Au-4f7/2 and 4f5/2 (Fig. 2e) [35,36]. The relationship between the two binding energies of Au4f is 3.50 eV obviously confirms the metallic nature of Au [35,36].

Fig. 1 shows the HR-TEM images of MFOF, GQDs-MFOF and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF. The HR-TEM images show a uniform layer with sheet-like morphological structures. These layers can serve as potential support material for the incorporation of various functional nanomaterials (Fig. 1c). In addition, GQDs-MFOF shows sheet-like morphological structures with an incorporated small dotted particles (GQDs). The obtained particle size diameters are slightly larger than those of GQDs may due to their substrate effect of MFOF (Fig. 1d). Finally, a well distinguishable AuNPs were formed in the sheet-like structure of the MFOF. The obtained particle size diameter was found to be ∼12.45 ± 0.09 nm and this result clearly indicated that the sheet-like structure of MFOF was not affected after the introduction of AuNPs (Fig. 1e and f). The SAED pattern of HR-TEM image result revealed the highly crystalline nature of synthesized GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF (Fig. S4). The composition of nanomaterial was evaluated by elemental mapping and detailed results are given in ESI (Fig. S5).

A detailed description of electrode fabrication procedure was included in ESI. SI.4. Fig. S6 shows the CV at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE in PBS (pH 7.0) at 50 mV/s. No signals were detected at the bare GC, MFOF, and GQDs-MFOF/GCEs, there are no electroactive redox sites. The GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE showed a sharp redox peak at E pa = +0.91 V and E pc = +0.45 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) due to the characteristic peak of gold oxide [35]. Furthermore, the interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct) of different modified electrodes surface was analyzed by electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Fig. S7 shows the Nyquist plots for control GC, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF, and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs in Fe(CN)6 3−/4− (0.1 M KCl) at a constant sampling frequency. The obtained RCT values are 355, 274, 202 and 85 Ω for GC, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs, respectively. The heterogeneous electron transfer rate constant (k et) value of modified electrode was calculated using standard Eq. (1) [25].
(1)
k
e t
= R T /
n
2
F
2
A
R
C T
C
0
where, R, T, A and C are usual meaning. The calculated k et are 0.89, 1.02, 1.84, and 2.96 × 10−4 cm2 s−1 for GC, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF, and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs, respectively. Compared with all electrodes, the GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF showed a lower RCT with 3.3-fold higher k et value, confirmed that rate of the electrode reaction is feasible. Furthermore, electroactive surface area (EASA) of the nanocomposite modified electrodes was calculated with help of Randles-Sevcik Eq. (2) [25].
(2)
i p
= 2.69 ×
10
5
n
3 / 2
A
D
1 / 2
C
υ
1 / 2
where, A, D and C are usual meaning. The calculated EASA are 0.062, 0.084, 0.158, and 0.326 cm2 for bare GC, MFOF, GQDs-MFOF, and GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs, respectively. The nanocomposite modified electrode surface about 5.2-fold times higher values than control GCE.

The electrocatalytic oxidation response of l-Cys-at modified electrode surface was initially analyzed by CVs and DPVs. Fig. 3 A shows the CVs for 200 µM of l-Cys-at nanocomposite modified electrode at a scan rate of 50 mV/s (vs. Ag/AgCl). No current signal was observed at bare GCE within the active potential window (−0.2 to +0.6 V). The MFOF, and GQDs-MFOF/GCEs exhibited broad oxidation peaks at +0.45 V and +0.36 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), with peak current of L-Cys I pa = 13.37 and 19.46 µA, respectively (Fig. 3A. curves b and c). Interestingly, a sharp irreversible peak delivered at E pa = +0.42 V with current of I pa = 35.85 µA at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE. As we known that continuous potential cycles can lead to the adsorption of CyS*ads (intermediate species undergoing Cys-oxidation) at electrode surface and to mask the flow of electron (ie. electrode fouling process). As compared to the first and tenth potential cycles (curve d) (dotted line), the GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF electrode surface does not affect the oxidation peak behavior of l-Cys. Curve e shows that no current response was observed in the absence of l-Cys.

Nanocomposite modified electrode has higher and stable current responses, which may responsible for their superior combination and intrinsic properties of AuNPs and MFOF, GQDs, as well as their ease of chemical reaction. In addition, the obtained catalytic response of l-Cys-are closely related to the electro active surface area (EASA) of the modified electrodes (Fig. S8). Fig. S9A shows the effect of scan rates (10–100 mV/s) with respect to 200 µM of l-Cys. The scanning rate increases with increasing the current response of l-Cys. The plot result shows the oxidation current response of l-Cys-is linear against square root of the scan rate and regression coefficient equation expressed as I pa (µA) = (0.1018) [L-Cys] (µM) + (3.5618) with R2 = 0.9956 (Fig. S9B), indicating the oxidation behavior of l-Cys-is a diffusion-controlled process.

An influence of the electrolytic supporting medium (pH) is an important parameter for electrochemical sensor because it provides detailed information about the peak shape, potential, and redox mechanism of target analyte. Fig. S10 shows the DPVs for 2 µM of l-Cys-at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE in PBS containing pH solutions from 4.0 to 8.0. The obtained results show that when pH of the solution was increased from 4.0 to 7.0, the oxidation current response of l-Cys-also increased and peak potential was shifted towards the less positive. When the working solution pH was increased to 8.0, the current response was decreased than pH 7.0. The linear potential shift revels that protons are directly involved in the oxidation process of Cys-and the plausible mechanism is as follows Eqs. (3)–(5) [36].
(3)
C y S H ↔ C y
S
−
+
H
+
(4)
C y
S
−
→ C y S +
e
−
(5)
2 C y S → C y S S y C

The obtained results clearly show that among the different pH solutions, pH 7.0 was optimized because it provided a higher current with sharp oxidation peak. Moreover, pH 7.0 was closely related to the physiological environment and it was directly used for all the biological applications.

Fig. 4 A shows the DPV for 2 µM of l-Cys-at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE in 0.2 M PBS (pH 7.0). The sharp peak at E pa = +0.21 V while the initial addition of 2 µM of l-Cys (curve a). Furthermore, the concentrations of l-Cys-was increased with increasing current responses and without significant change of peak potential shift. The concentrations of l-Cys-varied from 4 to 56 µM and the oxidation current responses were gradually increased (curves b to n). The plot drawing between the current against concentration of l-Cys-is linear and regression coefficient equation expressed as I pa (µA) = (0.4155) [L-Cys] (µM) + (0.4391) with R2 = 0.9984 (Fig. 4B).

In addition, wide linear range concentrations of l-Cys-was analyzed by amp i-t curve. Fig. 5 A shows the current response of l-Cys-at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GC electrode under a constant dynamic mode (optimized applied potential +0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl); sample interval 50 s). The oxidation current signal was increased by the initial addition of l-Cys (3 nM) (curve a).

As the concentration of l-Cys-was increased from 6 nM to 36 nM, the oxidation current signals were increased gradually without any significant noise. The plot of current vs. concentration of l-Cys-is linear with a regression coefficient equation expressed as I pa (µA) = (1.5586) [L-Cys] (µM) + (1.5052) and R2 = 0.9991 (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, wide detection range concentrations of l-Cys-was evaluated by amp i-t curve (Fig. 5C and D). The concentration of l-Cys-was increased from 1 nM to 0.5 mM with increasing current responses of l-Cys. The plot of current against concentration of l-Cys-is linear with the regression coefficient equation expressed as I pa (µA) = (3.1212) [L-Cys] (µM) + (0.0622) and R2 = 0.9947 (Fig. 5D inset). In addition, the LOD and LOQ was calculated using the standard equation of 3σ/slope (S/N = 3) and (S/N = 10) [25,37,38,39]. The calculated LOD, LOQ and sensitivity are 0.124 nM, 0.863 nM and 2.364 μA μM−1 cm−2 for l-Cys. The obtained catalytic results of l-Cys-with the present sensor are compared with those of the existing reports and discussed in detail in Table 1 [7,15–17,19–22,35]. Among the different nanocomposites modified electrochemical sensors, the present work shows the superior electrocatalytic activity and live-cell application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of selectivity, stability, and reproducibility
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5E shows the amp i-t curve for the selective detection of l-Cys-at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF in other possible interfering biomolecules, amino acids and metal ions, respectively. Initially, the modified electrode surface was optimized for stable background current before the addition of target analyte. The current response was observed by the initial addition of l-Cys (5 nM) (curve a). Then, 10-fold higher concentrations of other sulfur, and non-sulfur-containing amino acids and biomolecules such as homocysteine (curve b), glutathione (curve c), tyrosine (curve d), glucose (curve e), urea (curve f), thiourea (curve g), and tryptophan (curve h) were added, very quiet current responses were observed, especially in curves b and c. Then, the current response of l-Cys-was again proof by adding 5 nM of l-Cys-in the same electrolyte mixture. Interestingly, the current signal response was again increased (curve a). Even, 50-fold higher concentrations of various metal cations and anions like Na+, K+, Cd2+, Zn2+, NO3 −, and SO4 2− (curves i-n) were added, no current signal was enhanced. Finally, a nanomolar concentration of l-Cys (5 nM) was added to the various mixture containing electrolytic solution and the current response was increased again without any interference (curve a). The obtained interference results clearly showed that the present sensor has potentially applied for selective detection of l-Cys. A detailed description of spectral and their corresponding results for stability, reproducibility and repeatability of l-Cys-were given in the ESI (Figs. S11 and 12) (SI. 5 and SI. 6). Furthermore, the physical stability of the synthesized material and chemical stability of the modified electrode was analyzed and their results were included in ESI (Fig. S13) (SI. 7).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in whole blood samples
ce:section.ce:para: From a clinical point of view, low concentrations of Cys-are directly connected to the various biological functions in living system. Therefore, the quantitative determination of Cys-in human whole blood samples is of a great importance. Human blood samples were collected from healthy individuals in a renowned clinical laboratory (the detailed blood sample collection procedures are included in ESI. SI.1). The analysis of Cys-in whole blood samples (two individuals) was monitored by DPV. Fig. 6 A, and B show the DPV signals at GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE in the absence and presence of human whole blood samples (15% v/v) in PBS (pH 7.0). In absence of real sample, no current response was observed within the working potential scale between −0.10 to +0.60 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) (curve a) and signals was made at E pa = +0.21, and 0.22 V, which may due to oxidation current of Cys. When, 2 µM of l-Cys-was added to the same mixture of solution, the signal responses were increased at a similar peak potential. The obtained concentration results of Cys-are good agreement with the reported method [40]. Moreover, other biological fluids such as fetal bovine serum (FBS) and cat blood serum (CBS) was also used for real sample analysis via standard addition method. A detailed description of found and recovery results of l-Cys-are given in Table S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time practical application in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: The kinetic behavior of endogenous production of cellular Cys-was monitored up to 300 min under an optimized working parameter by amp i-t curve (Fig. S14A). No current responses were obtained alone for E. coli W3110, serine and NaHS (curves a, b and c), and current signal was observed when serine and NaHS added to live cells containing LB medium (curve d). Fig. S14B shows the production of cellular Cys-as a function of contact time for 300 min. There is no current reaction in the single system (curves a, b and c), signal was enhanced by the addition of serine and NaHS to E. coli W3110 (curve d), and stable current response was performed at 25 min with I pa = 0.22 µA. While the current responses was gradually increased as a function of contact time up to 300 min (0.85 µA), no significant signal was observed at the end of 300 min. Subsequently, the effect of scavenger on the inhibition of Cys-production was monitored. A known amount of NEM (100 µM) was added at 100 min of the reaction carrier. The current response was dropped, followed by a gradual decrease up to 300 min (0.54 µA to 0.05 µA) (curve e). The above results clearly indicate that the present nanocomposite modified system was successfully used for real-time detection of cellular Cys-in live cells.

In summary, GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF has been successfully designed and then used for endogenous real-time cellular cysteine and sensitive detection of l-Cys. The synthesized nanocomposite was analyzed and confirmed by spectral (FT-IR, XRD, and XPS), microscopic (HR-TEM) and electrochemical techniques (CVs, DPVs and amp i-t curve). The catalytic response of the different modified electrodes was expressed as GC ˂ MFOF ˂ GQDs-MFOF ˂ GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCEs. The GQDs-AuNPs-MFOF/GCE delivered a superior lower detection limit (0.124 nM) from their wide range of concentrations (1 nM to 0.5 mM) and LOQ of 0.863 nM. The nanocomposite probe has been effectively applied for the quantitative determination of Cys-in human whole blood and other bio-fluid samples. Additionally, the nanocomposite modified system was able to monitor the real-time endogenous quantification of cellular Cys-from live cells with help of an effective stimulators. Finally, the present sensor has significant electrochemical advantages such as high stability, reproducibility, ease of sensor preparation, and signal repeatability.

P. Arul: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sheng-Tung Huang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Veerappan Mani: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Chih-Hung Huang: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST-107–2113-M-027–006 and MOST-108–2113-M-027–001). P. Arul would like to gratitude National Taipei University of Technology for the position of Research Assistant Professor.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2022.140937.
